text.title: Multi-Agent Systems — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the multi-agent-systems methodology. Sources: Anthropic — Building Effective Agents; Park et al. — Generative Agents (arXiv:2304.03442); LangGraph state-machine docs.

rule.statement: Pick the framework by decision rule: state-machine + branching → LangGraph; role-played agents + comms → CrewAI; conversational mesh → AutoGen; flat pipeline → custom orchestrator with a queue.
rule.rationale: Frameworks have different mental models; picking the wrong one produces 2–3× more glue code than the alternative.

rule.statement: Every cross-agent message is a typed object (Pydantic / TypeBox / Zod): sender, recipient, payload-schema-id, payload, trace-id; never raw strings.
rule.rationale: Typed messages are the only mechanical defence against silent context drift, and the only way to replay traces deterministically.

rule.statement: Express the workflow as an explicit state machine (or LangGraph) before writing agent code; the graph is the contract, the agents are pluggable.
rule.rationale: Without a graph, multi-agent debugging degenerates into reading chat logs; with a graph, regressions are localised to one edge.

rule.statement: Set a per-run cumulative-cost budget (tokens, dollar, wall-clock) and a per-agent step budget; emergency-stop when any budget is exceeded.
rule.rationale: Multi-agent systems amplify cost; a runaway loop without budgets can spend $100s of dollars in minutes.

rule.statement: Wire OpenTelemetry traces for every agent step + edge before shipping; log model id, prompt id, latency, cost, and outcome; replay must be possible from logs alone.
rule.rationale: Without traces, multi-agent systems become unreproducible; production incidents have no signal to diagnose.
